notable.notable_blurb: The Learned Blacksmith; pacifist, abolitionist, US Consul; founded

narrative_blocks.body: He launched the League of Universal Brotherhood in 1846 and convened the first International Peace Congress in Brussels two years later. Victor Hugo presided over the Paris congress that followed.

narrative_blocks.header: Taught himself thirty languages while shoeing horses
narrative_blocks.body: He worked at a New Britain forge from his teens, studying languages from Latin and Greek to Hebrew and Sanskrit. Massachusetts governor Edward Everett dubbed him 'the Learned Blacksmith,' a title Burritt disliked.

narrative_blocks.header: Lincoln appointed him U.S. Consul to Birmingham
narrative_blocks.body: He served the British industrial city from 1864 to 1868. He used the post to advocate transatlantic peace negotiations during and after the Civil War.

narrative_blocks.header: Read Emma Willard's school reform plan aloud
narrative_blocks.body: On March 18, 1840 his cousin's reform proposal was presented at Kensington Congregational Church through him, because women could not speak publicly. The crowd listened 'with deep and thrilling interest.'

narrative_blocks.header: Hinsdale family connects him to the Hooker tree
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather Elijah Hinsdale was the brother of Lydia Hinsdale, who married into the Hart-Hooker line. Burritt was first cousin once removed of Emma Willard and Almira Hart Phelps.

cross_connections.display_label: Read her address at Kensington church — she could not speak publicly

INST051.name: League of Universal Brotherhood
INST051.notes: Founded by Elihu Burritt (X01932) in 1846. International peace organization. Launched at Pershore, England; supported by Joseph Sturge, James Silk Buckingham, and John Jefferson of the London Peace Society. Published Bond of Brotherhood monthly. British branch merged with London Peace Society 1857. Influenced later UK peace movement.

crossConnections.display_label: Read her address at Kensington church — she could not speak publicly
